pages:
1 
 Breast cancer : Types , Stages and Grades  
Normal breast:   
Normal female breast is made up of lobes, lobules and milk ducts and stroma. Each breast 
consists of 15 -20 lobes. Each lobe is made up of milk producing glands which are known as 
lobules. These lobes and lobules are connected by milk ducts which carry milk  from the lobules 
to nipple. Lobes, lobules, ducts, blood vessels and lymphatic vessels are surrounded by fatty and 
connective tissue which is known as stroma (National Breast Can cer Foundation, 2015).  
Lymphatic system consists of lymph nodes and lymph vessels. Lymph nodes are made up of 
clusters of bean shaped immune system cells (that are important in fighting infections). Lymph 
nodes are connected to lymph vessels which are smal l veins that carry the clear fluid known as 
lymph away from the breast (National Breast Cancer Foundation, 2015).  
Cancer : 
Cancer is defined as uncontrolled division of abnormal cells in any part of the body (Tariq et al. 
,2013) . There are different types of cancers but all the cancers start due to unwanted grow th of 
abnormal cells . In a normal cell cycle, normal cells start to proliferate and differentiate into new 
cells and when  cells complete their life span or they are damaged, they are killed by differen t 
process and new cells are formed. In cancer, cell cycle does not work properly, and new cells are 
formed continuously. In cancer cell growth is higher than cell death. These cells start to 
accumulate and form a mass of tissue which is known as tumor. Mos t of cancers form solid 
tumor but some cancers like leukemia do not form solid tumor. There are two types of the tumors 
i.e.  benign and malignant. Benign tumor does not have ability to invade surrounding tissue and 
cannot metastasize however when they bec ome very large they can cause problems. Once 
removed usually benign tumor cannot grow again. Tumors which are cancerous are known as 
malignant tumors. Who has the ability to invade surrounding tissue and can spread to other parts 
of the body through blood and lymph node (American Cancer S ociety , 2015). 
All over the world cancer has major impact on the society. Worldwide, 10 million new cases of 
cancer occur every year. In 2012 14.1 million new cases of cancer were detected. Worldwide in 
2012, lung cancer, f emale breast cancer, colorectal cancer and stomach cancer were more than

2 
 40% of all the cancers. In men the most common cancer was lung cancer with incidence of 
16.7% of all the new cases whereas in women breast cancer was the most common cancer with 
the incidence of 25.2% of all the new cases. In 2012, cancer accounted for 8.2 million deaths 
worldwide (WHO,  2014) .  
Breast cancer:  
Cancer that originates from the tissue of breast is known as breast cancer. Breast cancer can 
originate from inner lining of milk duct, lobules and from other areas of breast (Shaukat et al., 
2013) . In the United State breast cancer is accounted for 1 in 3 cancers. After lung cancer breast 
cancer is the second leading cause of cancer related deaths among t he women (Desantis et al. , 
2014) . In 2008 breast cancer accounted for 23% (1.38 million) of all the new cancer cases and it 
accounted for 14% (458,400) of all the cancer related death s. About half of the br east cancer 
cases and 60% of all the breast cancer deaths occur in the developing countries. The high 
incidence of breast cancer is found in Australia, western and northern Europe, North America; 
intermediate in Northern Africa, south America ; and low in Africa and Asia (Jemal et al. , 2011) . 
In Pakistan breast cancer is the life threading disease of wome n(Shamsi et al. , 2013) . In Pakistan, 
breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed cancer among the women. In Pakistan breast 
cancer is found in every one in nine female pat ients and incidence is 2.5 times higher in Pakistan 
than in Iran and India (Asif et a l., 2014) . In Asia highest breast cancer incidence is found in 
Pakistan except that in Israel (Butt et al. , 2010) . 
Types o f breast cancer:  
Breast cancer is divided into two broad categories; noninvasive  and invasive breast cancer. In 
noninvasive  or in situ breast cancer, cancerous cells remain localized to the ducts and do not 
spread to the fatty and connective tissue of the breast where as in invasive or infiltrating breast 
cancer cancerous cells spread to fatty and connective tissue of breast and other parts of body 
(Sharma G N et al., 2010).  
Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS):

3 
 Ductal carcinoma in situ is also known as in sit u, preinvasive and non invasive breast cancer. In 
DCIS malignant ductal epithelial cells remain in the ducts and do not invade the surrounding 
fatty and connective tissue of breast (barnes N L P et al.,  2012).  
Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC):  
IDC is also  known as infiltrating ductal carcinoma. In IDC, cancer cells accumulate in the milk 
duct and these cells have the ability to invade surrounding tissue of the breast. These cells can 
also metastasize to distant parts of the body through blood and lymph nod es. Most common type 
of breast cancer is IDC which accounts for 80% of diagnosed breast cancer cases.  
Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC):  
ILC is also known as infiltrating lobular carcinoma. ILC starts in the milk producing glands 
which are known as lobule. It has the ability to invade surrounding tissue and also can 
metastasize to other parts of the body. It is the second most common type of invasive breast 
cancer. It accounts for about 10% to 15% of all the invasive breast cancers (Sharma G N et al., 
2010)  
Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC):  
IBC is a most aggressive type of breast cancer. IBC accounts for 2.5% of all the cases of breast 
cancer. In IBC skin becomes red and warm and thick. IBC patients present with pain, and red, 
tender and enlarged breast (Uden  van D J P et al., 2014).  
Paget disease of nipple:  
Paget disease of nipple starts in the milk duct from where cancer cells invade nipple and areola. 
Paget disease is rare accounting for 1% of all the breast cancer cases (Sharma G N et al 2010).  
Triple ne gative breast cancer:  
Triple negative breast cancer is negative for estrogen, progesterone and Her -2 receptors. 
Therefore hormone therapy and Her -2 targeted therapy cannot be used to treat this cancer ( Reddy  
K.B 2011).

4 
  
Breast cancer staging:  
Cancer staging is a process which determines severity of cancer and it is performed on the basis 
of magnitude of primary cancer and cancer metastasis. To determine the stage of a cancer 
various tests are used which are as follows:  
Physical examination pro vides information about location, size, regional lymph nodes 
involvement and metastasis of cancer.  
Imaging tests :  X-ray, MRI and CT scans provide information about location and size of 
tumor.  
Pathologic report provides information about tumor size, growt h of tumor in the surrounding 
tissue and also helps to determine type of cancer cells and grade of tumor.  
Surgical report: After surgery sample is used to determine the appearance and size of tumor and 
is used to determine involvement of lymph nodes ( Ameri can Joint Committee on C ancer , 
2015 ). 
TNM staging:  
TNM stands for tumor, node and metastasis. In TNM system cancer is classified on the basis of 
tumor size, involvement of lymph nodes and metastasis. TNM staging system includes five 
stages (0 to IV). Stage  0 is used for noninvasive DCIS and stage I to stage IV is used for invasive 
breast cancer.  
T category describes:  
TX means no primary tumor assessed T0 means no evidence of primary tumor, Tis means 
carcinoma in situ, T1 to T4 means size of primary tumor.  
N category:  
Nix means regional lymph node cannot be assessed, N0 means no involvement of regional 
lymph node, and N1 to N3 means involvement of regional lymph nodes.

5 
  
M category:  
M0 means no metastasis, M1 means cancer has spread to other par ts of the body (Cancer.Net) . 
Stage0:  this stage is used to describe DCIS and Paget disease of nipple. In this stage regional 
lymph nodes are not involved.  
Stage I:  This stage is divided into IA and IB. In IA tumor size is 2cm [T1] and tumor is not 
spread to the regional lymph nodes [N0] and distant parts of the body [M0]. In IB tumor is either 
not found or tumor size is 2cm [T0 or T1], with involvement of axillary lymph nodes [N1mi] but 
cancer has not spread to the distant organs [M 0] (National Cancer Society, 2015) . 
Stag e II: Stage II is further divided into two types known as stage IIA and stage IIB. In stage IIA 
tumor is either less than 2cm with under arm lymph nodes involvement  or tumor size is larger 
than 2cm but less than 5cm and under arm lymph nodes are not invol ved or tumor not found in 
the breast but cancer cells are found in the 1 -3 under arm lymph nodes and in the internal 
mammary lymph nodes. Stage IIB tumor size is either more than 2cm but less than 5cm with the 
involvement of under arm lymph nodes or intern al mammary lymph nodes or tumor size is more 
than 5cm without the involvement of under arm lymph nodes.  
Stage III:  Stage 3 is further classified into three groups i.e. stage 3A, stage 3B, and stage 3C.  
Stage 3A:  Stage 3A is further classified into three su b groups  
Tumor size is more than 2cm but less than 5cm with the involvement of 4 -9 under arm lymph 
nodes or internal mammary lymph nodes or the tumor size is more than 5cm with the 
involvement of under arm lymph nodes or internal mammary lymph nodes or tum or not found in 
the breast but cancer cell are present in the 4 -9 under arm lymph nodes or internal mammary 
lymph nodes.  
Stage 3B:  Tumor is either fixed to the skin or chest wall without the involvement of under arm 
lymph nodes or tumor is either fixed to the skin or chest wall with the involvement of up to 9 
under arm lymph nodes or internal mammary lymph nodes.

6 
 Stage 3c:  Tumor is of any size with the involvement of more than 10 under arm lymph nodes 
and internal mammary lymph nodes or lymph nodes above an d below the collarbone.  
Stage 4:  tumor is of any size, lymph nodes may or may not be involved but cancer has 
metastasized to other parts of the body such as bone, lung, liver, brain and distant lymph  nodes 
(Breast Cancer Care, 2015) . 
Breast cancer grade:  
Cancer cells are given a grade when they are observed under the microscope. The grade is based 
on how different they are to the normal breast cells and their growth. There are three grades of 
breast cancer  
Grade 1 or well differentiated: Cells look more li ke normal cells and grow slowly.  
Grade 2 or moderately differentiated: cells look less like normal cells and grow faster  
Grade 3 or poorly differentiated: cells look different from normal cells and will grow and spread 
faster

7

 [Accessed 16 Nov 2015].
